retrieved_docs.title: Status Epilepticus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Refractory status epilepticus should be treated with a continuous infusion of an antiepileptic drug. Choices include an intravenous (IV) infusion of midazolam, pentobarbital, thiopental, or propofol (propofol infusion should not be used in children due to the risk of propofol infusion syndrome).

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations in Electroconvulsive Therapy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Sevoflurane is an option for induction of anesthesia in ECT. It has a slow onset compared to intravenous anesthetics. It can be helpful to initiate anesthesia to obtain IV access on a patient who is uncooperative awake. When used for a short period, sevoflurane has a quick recovery. In a systematic review, sevoflurane reduces seizure length more prominently than propofol, barbiturates, and ketamine. [26] Sevoflurane does not attenuate the sympathetic stimulation to the extent as other induction agents, so opioids or beta-blockers are often used to maintain hemodynamic stability. [27] Sevoflurane enhances muscular blockade compared to intravenous anesthetics.

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Barbiturates are a category of sedative-hypnotic medications used for treating seizure disorders, neonatal withdrawal, insomnia, preoperative anxiety, and the induction of coma to address increased intracranial pressure (ICP). In addition, these medications are also helpful for inducing anesthesia. Thiopental was introduced in 1934 for general anesthesia induction. [1] This drug was the predominant intravenous (IV) anesthetic induction agent until propofol replaced it. [2] In refractory status epilepticus, a Cochrane Review found that thiopental was equally effective as propofol for controlling seizure activity. [3] In 2011, the primary supplier of thiopental chose to cease its production due to its involvement in lethal injection procedures, resulting in its unavailability in the United States. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations in Electroconvulsive Therapy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Propofol also acts as a GABA agonist. However, it tends to shorten seizure duration as it increases the seizure threshold. It is used in ECT as an anesthetic agent when previous seizures induced by ECTs have been prolonged and when a rapid onset is needed. Propofol is dosed at 1.5 to 2 mg/kg. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Ictal-Interictal Continuum -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A benzodiazepine with rapid onset, such as intramuscular midazolam, intravenous lorazepam, or intravenous diazepam, can be considered a first-line treatment. Alternatively, the introduction of a nonsedating antiseizure medication that can be rapidly loaded intravenously, such as levetiracetam or valproic acid, may be used. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Inhalational Anesthetic -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The use of inhaled agents has also demonstrated benefits in the critical care setting, where they are often utilized in conjunction with intravenous anesthetics to provide optimal sedation for urgent procedural interventions. Their role outside of the operating room has increased as procedural suites outside the traditional operating room have also increased. Inhalational anesthetic agents have been used more in medically refractory epilepsy patients in neurointensive care units. These agents have also been used to treat status asthmaticus refractory to medical management. In this setting, physician assistants, nurse practitioners, critical care nurses, and ICU staff should have a fundamental knowledge of inhaled anesthetic agents. It is important to have essential care staff abreast of inhaled agents, and signs and symptoms of complications since this staff often see these patients in the postoperative anesthesia care unit or intensive care unit.

retrieved_docs.title: New Onset Refractory Status Epilepticus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The International League Against Epilepsy (ILAE) defines status epilepticus (SE) as continuous clinical and/or electrographic seizure activity, or recurrent seizure activity without recovery to baseline, lasting for ≥ 5 mins. [1] If the seizure activity continues despite treatment with adequate doses of an initial benzodiazepine and an acceptable second-line intravenous (IV) antiepileptic medication, the patient is considered to have progressed into refractory status epilepticus (RSE). [2] Super refractory status epilepticus (SRSE) is defined as RSE that continues or recurs despite the use of IV anesthetic agents for ≥ 24 hours, including those cases that recur upon withdrawal or cessation of anesthetic agents. [3] RSE is seen in approximately 9% to 43% of all SE cases, with a reported in-hospital mortality of 15% to 33%. [4] [5] It has been observed that approximately 4% to 12% of patients with SE eventually progress to SRSE, with in-hospital mortality reported to be as high as 40% to 54%. [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthetic Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The symptoms of LAST may closely resemble other perioperative complications or be obscured by sedation, so care must be taken to ensure timely diagnosis. Seizures may be caused by epilepsy or other causes of seizures, such as hypoglycemia, other electrolyte abnormalities, or infections. Arrhythmias may be caused by the patient’s underlying cardiac pathology, channelopathies, medication-induced QTc prolongation, or other causes.

retrieved_docs.title: Etomidate -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: The most common adverse reaction associated with etomidate use is transient intravenous pain on injection. [11] The pain appears less frequent when larger, more proximal arm veins are used, or IV lidocaine is given before an etomidate bolus. Transient skeletal muscle movements or myoclonus were observed in about 32% of the patients. Electroencephalographic studies have failed to expose seizure activity and suggest that these muscle movements demonstrate the disinhibition of cortical activity. [12] [13] Severe, disturbing movements were decreased when 0.1 mg of fentanyl was given immediately before induction. [14] According to several studies, pretreatment with midazolam, dexmedetomidine, narcotics, propofol, dezocine, or ketamine reduces myoclonus incidence and severity after a bolus dose of etomidate. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Electroconvulsive Therapy -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: ECT utilizes general anesthesia. Anesthetic induction medications used include barbiturates such as thiopental and methohexital and nonbarbiturate agents such as propofol and etomidate. Seizure-induced by ECT should last longer than 30 seconds. Methohexital is the most commonly used induction agent due to its quick onset, effectiveness, low cost, and minimal effect on seizure duration. Propofol and thiopental have been shown to reduce seizure duration. Etomidate has correlations with myoclonus and increased seizure duration.

retrieved_docs.title: New Onset Refractory Status Epilepticus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Intravenous (IV) benzodiazepines are the agents of choice for aborting seizures emergently. The use of benzodiazepines in the prehospital setting has been shown to terminate seizures in 43% to 59% cases with SE, en-route to the hospital, with less need for respiratory support on arrival to the emergency department and need for ICU subsequently. Intramuscular (IM) midazolam has been observed to be superior and more efficacious than IV lorazepam in patients without IV access. [33] However, IM midazolam, IV lorazepam, IV diazepam, and IV phenobarbital have been recommended as initial drugs of choice for aborting seizures lasting at least 5 minutes, with similar efficacy. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations in Electroconvulsive Therapy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pharmacology of methohexital in the setting of electroconvulsive therapy. Explain the reasoning behind providing paralysis in electroconvulsive therapy. Identify the most common adverse advents associated with electroconvulsive therapy. Summarize the induction, maintenance, and emergence of anesthesia for electroconvulsive therapy by interprofessional tea. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: New Onset Refractory Status Epilepticus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Current guidelines recommend using continuous IV infusion of anesthetic agents for seizure suppression or burst suppression coma for 24-48 hours before attempting to wean. [2] Midazolam: Load with 0.2 mg/kg IV followed by continuous infusion at 0.05 to 2 mg/kg/hr. Propofol: 2 mg/kg IV bolus followed by maintenance infusion at 20 to 250 mcg/kg/min. Pentobarbital: 5 mg/kg IV bolus, continuous infusion at 1 to 10 mg/kg/hr. Ketamine: Load with 0.5-4 mg/kg followed by maintenance infusion at 0.3 to 5 mg/kg/hr. IV midazolam and propofol are preferred over longer-acting agents like pentobarbital as long-acting agents are associated with a prolonged need for mechanical ventilation and cardiovascular complications. [35] [36] However, higher doses and prolonged treatment of propofol have been associated with propofol infusion syndrome leading to higher mortality and complications than midazolam and ketamine.

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: Pentobarbital: Pentobarbital is administered by IM and IV routes. Pentobarbital is administered by IM route 150 mg to 200 mg as a single-dose pre-anesthetic medication. For status epilepticus, pentobarbital is given as a 5 to 15 mg/kg IV loading dose followed by a continuous infusion of 0.5 to 5 mg/kg/h. Continuous infusion dosing of pentobarbital is titrated electroencephalogram (EEG), following the guidelines set by the Neurocritical Care Society. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Midazolam -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Midazolam is a short-acting benzodiazepine frequently used for anesthesia induction, seizure management, and procedural sedation. The water-soluble formulation allows for rapid onset and flexible administration routes, including intravenous and intramuscular, making it suitable for managing status epilepticus when intravenous access is limited. Midazolam also provides anxiolysis and hypnosis during the maintenance phase of general anesthesia and serves as an adjunct to regional and local anesthesia in various diagnostic and therapeutic procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations in Patients With Cerebral Palsy -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Preoperative assessment should be comprehensive, and a multidisciplinary approach may be beneficial depending on the severity of a patient’s symptoms. Caregivers are a crucial source of information for obtaining an adequate history. Medications for seizures, spasticity, and GERD should be continued perioperatively. Preoperative anxiety should be carefully assessed, and premedication with an anxiolytic may be administered if indicated. Establishing intravenous access can be difficult due to dehydration, chronic spasticity, and lack of patient cooperation. Topical application of a eutectic mixture of local anesthetics (EMLA) cream may be helpful for the uncooperative patient, and an additional assistant may be required. Inhalational induction followed by post-induction vascular access can be a reasonable option if clinically indicated. Ultrasound guidance can be a valuable tool if persistent difficulty is encountered. [4]

retrieved_docs.content: providing clinicians with a comprehensive framework for drug selection, dosing, metabolism considerations and potential drug‑drug interactions in the management of anxiety, sedation, muscle spasticity, pain, anesthesia and related neuropharmacologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 319
retrieved_docs.content: agent during anesthesia. Together, these terminologies deliver an integrated framework for identifying, classifying, and communicating about diverse pharmacologic and therapeutic substances across patient care and biomedical research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Methoxyfluran, a halogenated ether anesthetic, is recognized for its ability to induce فقدان الوعي (loss of consciousness), which is a central therapeutic objective in the administration of general anesthesia. By depressing central nervous system activity, methoxyfluran reliably produces the unconscious state required for surgical procedures, highlighting its role as an effective agent for achieving controlled loss of consciousness in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1638
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a diverse group of agents used to produce reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia for surgical procedures. The category “anesthetics, general” includes chloroform, enflurane, fencyclidine, flurothyl, isoflurane, methohexital, and propofol as child agents, each representing a distinct chemical class with specific pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic profiles. In addition, xenon—a noble gas—also falls under the classification of general anesthetics, indicating that it shares the core functional purpose of inducing general anesthesia despite its unique mechanism of action. Together, these agents illustrate the breadth of substances that can be employed to achieve controlled, reversible unconsciousness in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a diverse class of pharmacologic agents used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, amnesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical procedures. This class includes chloralosa, chloroform, enfluran, etomidát, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, ketamin, methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. Each of these agents functions as a “child” of the broader category of general anesthetics, reflecting their role as specific drugs that share the common purpose of producing and maintaining general anesthesia. While all serve the same overarching clinical goal, they differ in chemical structure, mechanism of action, potency, onset and duration of effect, and side‑effect profile, allowing clinicians to tailor anesthetic plans to the individual patient’s physiological status, surgical requirements, and risk factors. Understanding the full spectrum of these agents enhances patient safety, optimizes peri‑operative management, and supports informed selection of the most appropriate anesthetic regimen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2724
retrieved_docs.content: Chloralosa, diazepam, etomidát, and hexobarbital are all classified as hypnotics and sedatives. Each of these agents belongs to the drug class that produces sedation, promotes sleep, and can be used to manage anxiety or induce anesthesia in clinical settings. Their shared classification reflects common pharmacologic actions aimed at depressing central nervous system activity, making them useful in a variety of therapeutic contexts that require sedation or hypnotic effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: are frequently used together with the antiepileptic phenytoin, suggesting combined or concurrent use of opioids and antiepileptics in pain management for patients with seizure disorders. Mefobarbital is reported to treat the onset of a generalized tonic‑clonic seizure (نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة), a seizure type encompassed by the condition “epilepsy; convulsions,” which is linked to the cerebrum (velký mozek), highlighting cerebral involvement in convulsive epilepsy. Collectively, these entities and relationships provide clinicians with a comprehensive pharmacologic arsenal to individualize epilepsy management, address comorbid anxiety, and consider multimodal therapy involving opioids and other drug classes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2493
retrieved_docs.content: Mefobarbital may be used to treat generalized onset seizures, offering therapeutic benefit in controlling these seizure episodes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2805
retrieved_docs.content: Chloralosa, etomidát, and hexobarbital are all classified as hypnotics and sedatives. As members of this drug class, each agent is used to induce sleep, provide sedation, and produce anxiolysis in various clinical situations, such as managing insomnia, facilitating procedural sedation, and supporting anesthesia. Their shared categorization highlights a common pharmacologic profile and therapeutic purpose in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: The seizure type نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة is classified within the broader category of epilepsy; convulsions, indicating that it represents a generalized onset non‑motor seizure. In clinical practice, the barbiturate mefobarbital may be used to treat نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة, providing therapeutic benefit for patients who experience this specific form of seizure. Recognizing نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة as part of epilepsy; convulsions informs appropriate selection of mefobarbital in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics encompass a broad spectrum of agents that are classified under the category “anesthetics, general.” This group includes the inhalational agents chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, and flurothyl, as well as the intravenous agents methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. In addition, xenon is identified as a specific type of general anesthetic. Together, these substances represent the diverse pharmacologic options used to achieve reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia during surgical and procedural care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 394
retrieved_docs.content: GABA modulators are characterized as antagonists and inhibitory agents that can block or diminish GABA‑mediated neurotransmission. This pharmacologic profile encompasses a broad spectrum of compounds, including the volatile anesthetic gases chloroform, enflurane, halothane, isoflurane and nitrous oxide; the intravenous anesthetics etomidate, methohexital, propofol and bupivacaine; the barbiturates flurazepam and flurothyl; the dissociative agent fencyclidine; the hypnotic‑sedative chloralose; the carboxylic acids; the inert gas nitrous oxide; the mineral kaolin; as well as amitriptyline, thiorphan and other hypnotic‑sedative agents that are listed as allowed qualifiers for antagonist‑inhibitory actions. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in selecting drugs for anesthesia, sedation, analgesia and neuropsychiatric treatment and in anticipating therapeutic effects, drug interactions and adverse outcomes. Antagonists and inhibitors are frequently qualified by local‑anesthetic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1467
retrieved_docs.content: Bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics. These agents belong to the benzodiazepine family and share a common mechanism of enhancing the inhibitory effects of gamma‑aminobutyric acid (GABA) at the GABA‑A receptor, which produces anxiolytic, sedative, muscle‑relaxant, and anticonvulsant effects. Because they are members of the same therapeutic class, they are often considered together when selecting medication for anxiety disorders, procedural sedation, or related conditions, and their pharmacologic similarities guide dosing, monitoring, and the management of potential side‑effects in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1336
retrieved_docs.content: GABA modulátory are closely linked to the class of hypnotics and sedatives, reflecting their shared mechanism of enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. Flurazepam, a benzodiazepine, exemplifies this connection by acting as a GABA modulator while also being classified as a hypnotic and sedative, thereby producing sleep‑inducing and anxiolytic effects. Additional agents that belong to the hypnotics and sedatives category include chloralosa, etomidát, and hexobarbital, each of which exerts its sedative or anesthetic action through modulation of GABA‑ergic pathways or related mechanisms. Halothan is also related to hypnotics and sedatives, serving as an inhalational anesthetic with potent sedative properties. Together, these compounds illustrate how modulation of the GABA system underlies a broad spectrum of clinical agents used to induce sedation, hypnosis, or anesthesia, highlighting their relevance for patient management in perioperative and critical care settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1477
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a broad class of agents used to induce reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, amnesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical procedures. Within this class, chloroform, enfluran (enflurane), fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran (isoflurane), methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol are recognized as specific child agents that share the overarching function of producing general anesthesia. Xenon is also classified as a type of general anesthetic, reflecting its role as an inhalational agent with anesthetic properties. Although these agents differ in chemical structure, potency, onset and recovery times, and side‑effect profiles, they are all integrated into the therapeutic repertoire for achieving controlled, reversible unconsciousness in patients undergoing operative or diagnostic interventions. Understanding the relationships among these individual anesthetic agents and the general anesthetic category supports informed selection, dosing, and monitoring strategies to optimize patient safety and perioperative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2205
retrieved_docs.content: Mefobarbital may be used to treat نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة. Epilepsy; convulsions is classified as نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة and is typically found in the velký mozek. Recognizing that this seizure type originates in the cerebrum and can be managed with mefobarbital informs clinical assessment and therapeutic decision‑making.
